original_abstract:
Abstract

The landscape of gifted education across Europe is characterized by significant diversity, ranging from highly institutionalized systems to regions where the concept of giftedness remains largely unrecognized. This article provides a comparative overview of the educational frameworks, legislative provisions, and specialized support organizations for gifted children in ten European countries: the United Kingdom, Austria, Germany, Switzerland, Spain, Denmark, Finland, the Czech Republic, Hungary, and Slovakia. By mapping various school systems and identification methods, the study reveals a common tension between inclusive, integrated classroom models and segregated specialized programs. 

Evidence suggests that despite legal recognitions in many nations, actual implementation is often hindered by persistent myths—such as the belief that gifted education is inherently elitist or that high-potential children do not require specific interventions to succeed. The research highlights a critical disconnect where many current programs cater primarily to high achievers, frequently overlooking underachievers and those with asynchronous development. Furthermore, the analysis identifies a widespread lack of specialized teacher training as a major barrier to the effective nurturing of talent. As part of a broader holistic research project, this article emphasizes that without proactive identification and tailored pedagogical strategies, the unique potential of gifted children risks remaining untapped within general education systems.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract

The present article deals with the condition of gifted education in Europe. It turns out that there is a considerable difference between the countries, spanning from very developed systems to countries where the term “giftedness” has not yet gained ground. The research undertakes a comparative study of the educational systems and regulations in ten European countries (the United Kingdom, Austria, Germany, Switzerland, Spain, Denmark, Finland, the Czech Republic, Hungary, Slovakia), as well as the organizations and frameworks that provide additional resources for the gifted. It is a cartographic investigation focusing on the educational systems and the methods used to diagnose and evaluate the gifted children. The article also attempts to clarify the general controversy between the comprehensive-inclusive educational framework and the differentiated strategy of enrichment and accelerated learning through the separation of gifted students into independent educational frameworks.

Advocates for gifted and talented students have argued that laws enabling the advancement of such students are not being carried out because the concept of advanced learning has become the target of misleading propaganda. Examples of these false notions include the prevailing belief that advanced learning opportunities are elitist and the false notion that gifted and talented students do not require special support to succeed in school. The study examined the extent to which existing gifted and talented programs advance the learning of all types of G/T students and identified lack of appropriate teacher training as a major obstruction to the development of talent. This article is one of a series of reports emerging from a holistic, comprehensive research study focusing on the need for identification of and instruction strategies for the development of gifted and talented students within the context of general education. The absence of identification and appropriate instruction is a major factor impacting the potential of G/T students.
